data.title: Public Storage: Solid Cash Flow But Not A Buy Right Now
data.description:
The REIT class alone is not the most exciting in the world. It is generally for those looking for something more stable, without many surprises, but even so, within this world it is possible to find sectors such as logistics, data centers, or science, which have many more prospects and exciting aspects to analyze.

This is not quite the case with self-storage. It is not a sector that will revolutionize any trend, nor is it a REIT with several partners listed on the stock exchange. In fact, it is one of the simplest businesses there is: the company owns buildings and rents out space to people who need it.

At the top of this simplicity is Public Storage (NYSE:PSA), which, despite being a little boring, is very profitable and perhaps a little overvalued, which is why my rating is a “solid hold” rather than a buy.

Public Storage: Many Solid Points In This Investment Case

Simplicity can sometimes be linked to a lack of moats, but that is not quite the case with Public Storage. The company has several competitive edges that are important to mention.

The most obvious is scale. The company has more than 3,000 properties spread across the US, so it ends up building a brand and being a default choice for many customers. This is pure scale, as illustrated by its enterprise value of $65 billion, which places it among the largest REITs when we look at this KPI, and also makes it more than $20 billion larger than its main peer, Extra Space Storage (EXR).

Being much larger than its main peers, and still being in this more fragmented market, adds many practical advantages, not only in terms of diluting costs and expenses such as marketing, but also in terms of access to cheaper capital for expansion, the ability to grow through acquisitions, and so on.

Another important point to mention is the operational aspect of this REIT. PSA has high margins, and in principle, this can also be attributed to the business model, but credit must also be given to management's execution. As mentioned in the earnings call, the company is “the most efficient operating platform” in the industry. This is the result of initiatives such as investment in digital, customer experience, and well-executed acquisitions, as mentioned in the excerpt below:

“Public Storage's industry leadership is proven by, among other things, the highest revenue generation per square foot among peers, the most efficient operating platform, including customer and employee-centric technologies that are enhancing satisfaction while bolstering our revenue and margin advantages and the strongest ability to drive portfolio expansion through our best-in-class acquisition and development teams backed by our growth-oriented balance sheet. As the operating environment stabilizes, new competitive supply deliveries declined further and the transaction market becomes more active, we are holistically enhancing our advantages and positioning for growth.”

Finally, it is worth mentioning the balance sheet, which stands out when compared to the rest of the REIT industry. Debt to EBITDA ended at 3.1x in Q2, a very healthy level, especially when considering PSA's cash flow generation capacity.

Q2 Earnings And Value Creation

Q2 earnings were not that great, with super-accelerated growth or anything like that. But it serves to illustrate what I mentioned at the beginning of the analysis. Core FFO per share was $4.28, a small YoY increase of just over 1%, but with positive same-store revenue growth.

And the value creation engine continues to work, mainly through acquisitions and expansion. The 2020 properties, for example, already generate a yield of 12.4% (although growth has not been that interesting). This also makes me think that there is still a lot of room for growth through the maturation of the newer part of the portfolio, as the chart below suggests.

PSA Valuation: That’s Why It’s a Hold

The reason I don't give PSA a buy rating is precisely because of its valuation. It's not a particularly overvalued company, but it's not attractively priced either.

The price-to-FFO is 17.5x, which for a REIT that is not growing so rapidly is not that appealing. Compared to its peers (and other REIT segments), it is also not a multiple that allows for much upside and a high dividend yield. For instance, CubeSmart (CUBE) and National Storage Affiliates (NSA) trade at 15.4x and 13.8x, respectively. All of its peers also offer a higher forward dividend yield than PSA.

This is justified by the quality of the company, but in my view, it is too high a premium to pay at the moment.

The Bottom Line

PSA's execution is excellent, its business model is phenomenal, which makes its cash flow prospects and, consequently, its dividends very reliable.

At the same time, the valuation does not allow me to be so optimistic, mainly when we’re seeing so many quality REITs like VICI Properties (VICI) and Realty Income (O) trading at 12x - 13x FFO. Therefore, I prefer to leave the rating as a “solid hold” for now.

data.title: Gold Slips Below $4,000 As Dollar Strength And Easing Tensions Spark Profit-Taking
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya

Gold prices retreated on Thursday, dipping below the key $4,000 level after a sharp rally that pushed the metal to multi-year highs earlier this week. The pullback came as traders locked in profits and a rebound in the U.S. dollar index weighed on bullion demand.

The dollar gained 0.5% to reach a two-month high, making gold more expensive for foreign investors and temporarily halting its upward momentum. The easing of geopolitical tensions in the Middle East also reduced safe-haven inflows that had fueled the recent surge.

Technically, gold has encountered resistance near the upper boundary of its rising channel and is now consolidating toward the midline around $3,950. The broader structure, however, remains constructive.

Since late summer, gold has held firmly within an ascending channel pattern, with consistent higher lows supported by the 20-day and 50-day exponential moving averages (EMAs), now positioned at $3,988 and $3,926, respectively. These levels form a critical support zone that will determine whether the current pullback is a temporary correction or the start of a deeper retracement.

Technical cooling offers space for consolidation

Momentum indicators reflect a cooling of the earlier rally. The Relative Strength Index (RSI) has declined from overbought territory above 70 to around 46, indicating a reset in short-term momentum.

This decline suggests the market is working off its stretched conditions rather than signaling a breakdown. Analysts note that such cooling phases often precede renewed buying interest, particularly within a strong uptrend.

If prices fail to hold above the $3,950–$3,920 region, the next area of interest lies near $3,840, which aligns with the lower band of the channel. A steeper decline could see gold test $3,720, though this remains a less likely scenario given the continued strength in macro fundamentals.

On the upside, a rebound above $4,050 would reinforce bullish control and set the stage for a retest of $4,100 and potentially fresh record highs if global risk appetite weakens again.

Broader fundamentals remain supportive

Despite short-term volatility, gold’s longer-term outlook remains positive. The metal is up over 50% year-to-date, supported by sustained central bank purchases and persistent ETF inflows as investors hedge against macroeconomic uncertainty.

Expectations of further Federal Reserve rate cuts in the coming months add to gold’s appeal, as lower interest rates reduce the opportunity cost of holding non-yielding assets.

Geopolitical dynamics also continue to underpin gold’s safe-haven demand. While tensions have temporarily eased, market participants remain cautious amid unresolved fiscal challenges in the U.S. and global growth concerns.

Analysts suggest that any renewed escalation in risk sentiment could quickly restore buying momentum, particularly as structural demand from sovereign reserves and institutional hedgers remains robust.

Outlook

In the near term, the $3,950–$3,920 zone serves as the key battleground for gold’s next move. A successful defense of this level could attract renewed buying interest and keep the broader uptrend intact.

Conversely, a decisive break lower could trigger a correction toward $3,840 before buyers return. For now, the metal’s long-term bias remains bullish, supported by favorable macro conditions and ongoing demand from both retail and institutional investors.

Earlier analysis highlighted that gold’s climb above $4,000 signaled strong momentum but warned that overbought conditions could trigger a healthy retracement. That scenario has now materialized, with the pullback aligning with profit-taking rather than a structural reversal.

data.title: CorMedix: Challenging Long-Term Risk-Reward, Maintain Sell
data.description:
Introduction

CorMedix's stock (NASDAQ:CRMD) is up 59% since my Sell, rating in late March.

Despite progress, such as the company achieving its first profitable quarter, I was cautious about CorMedix's long-term prospects. Moreover, my discounted cash flow (DCF) model implied that CRMD was priced for ~20% Y/Y revenue growth and ~18% free cash flow (FCF) margins, which seemed unrealistic over a nine-year projection for a company that markets a catheter lock solution.

The following article explores what went wrong in my last analysis and CorMedix’s future in light of recent developments (e.g., DefenCath performance, Melinta acquisition).

Recent Developments

I'll admit it: CorMedix is off to a blistering start in 2025. There's no denying it. DefenCath's net revenue was $39.1 million in Q1 and $39.7 million in Q2. Both quarters were profitable, with net income of $20.6 million and $19.8 million in Q1 and Q2, respectively.

However, let’s take a step back. As implied by the company’s original Q2 estimate range and the actual result, DefenCath remains challenging to model. The beat versus the initial midpoint ($39.7M vs. $26.9M) was +47.6%. The beat versus the initial upper bound was +28.5%. Of course, I am not trying to argue that a beat is a “bad thing.” Rather, I am pointing out that investors should not “overfit” to these results.

What I got wrong in March was the near-term trajectory. 1H25 sell-in and LDO rollout were stronger and more durable than I (or management) anticipated. Moreover, limited visibility (recall that management had only guided for 1H) did not translate into an immediate fade. Lastly, the company has since diversified its offerings (reducing “single-asset” exposure) following the Melinta acquisition (more on this later).

DefenCath Structural Risks Remain Despite Strong Sell-In

That being said, core DefenCath risks remain (think inventory timing, customer concentration, price erosion, and reimbursement clocks). Moreover, an inherently onboarding-driven launch can swing in both directions. All in all, while the execution was there in 1H, durability remains a major unknown.

As for FY25 DefenCath revenue, management now expects revenue between $200 million and $250 million, with upside potential on LDO progress.

Looking ahead, we now have a fixed TDAPA window. CMS explicitly lists DefenCath (HCPCS J0911) as TDAPA-eligible from July 1, 2024, through June 30, 2026 (2 years).

Why is this important? I argue that TDAPA expiration will not only impact price, but could impact adoption and sell-in as well.

For example, assume 30 vials/month and $200 net/vial (DefenCath WAC is $249.99 per 3 mL, but net realized price varies due to rebates, discounts, etc.). Post-TDAPA, the price may drop 20% (revenue will also drop 20%). Also, if adoption among CVC patients drops from, say, 60% to 40% (lower utilization incentive post-TDAPA), revenue drops 33%. If you combine both, that results in a ~47% revenue decline on the outpatient book.

More on TDAPA's impact on adoption: There is precedent for products seeing decreased adoption post-TDAPA. For instance, in 2021, etelcalcetide (prescribed to hemodialysis patients to prevent complications of elevated parathyroid hormone) use "decreased substantially after TDAPA ended in January 2021, with most patients switching to cinacalcet." This was even despite etelcalcetide being associated with better outcomes. While DefenCath and etelcalcetide are different drug classes (systemic calcimimetic vs. catheter lock), the economic incentives are analogous. In fact, the authors concluded that:

These findings have US policy implications not only for etelcalcetide and parathyroid hormone but also for future development of innovative therapies in dialysis.

Of course, there are alternatives to DefenCath (e.g., heparin-only locks, citrate locks, ethanol locks, compounded taurolidine locks) and other ways to offset CRBSI risk (e.g., reduce catheter days, access-care bundle, targeted prophylaxis only).

However, the reimbursement clock is not DefenCath’s only risk. Q/Q print volatility will remain, and customer concentration will amplify swings. For instance, in 2024, one customer (presumably an LDO) made up 86% of total revenue.

Another consideration, especially with TDAPA, is that LDOs face reduced expiry risk when stocking DefenCath. This is because DefenCath "customers" can return the product near/past expiry (up to six months) if it's unutilized. So, this materially reduces "expiry risk" for dialysis chains stocking DefenCath. Think about it. If you know you're able to return a product without a problem, you're more incentivized to try and buy it!

Please note that I do not intend to come off as a DefenCath “doomer.” I just want people to understand that the strong 1H sell-in does not automatically equal durable demand. Moreover, with TDAPA ending June 30, 2026, price and utilization could take a hit. So, do not extrapolate Q1 and Q2 figures (net income included).

The Melinta Acquisition

Although pricey at 2.5x sales, CorMedix’s recent $300 million (in upfront consideration, please read PR for terms and financing) acquisition of Melinta Therapeutics effectively diversifies CorMedix’s offerings and smooths Q/Q DefenCath revenue volatility.

The deal gives CorMedix a ready-made hospital/acute-care portfolio, including products like Rezzayo (an anti-fungal), Baxdela (fluroquinolone for ABSSSI), and Toprol-XL (cardiovascular brand), among others. It also has many synergies (think cross-selling and channel leverage), so it makes a lot of sense on paper.

DefenCath’s last two quarters imply a $160 million sell-in annualized run rate. Melinta’s steady and dependable $100-110 million in core product revenue should help cushion DefenCath’s quarterly volatility.

With that said, hospital anti-infectives carry heavy GTN and modest organic growth. So, while CorMedix's revenue base gets an immediate "facelift," I wouldn't expect Melinta to meaningfully accelerate its overall growth.

The company is hopeful that Rezzayo's label can expand to include "prophylaxis of invasive fungal infections in adult patients undergoing allogeneic blood and marrow transplant." Moreover, it is still evaluating DefenCath in patients receiving total parenteral nutrition. CorMedix estimates that both indications (together) could add up to $400 million in peak annual sales.

Before we move on to "valuation assumptions," I have to point out that the acquisition really changes things. Think about it carefully. CorMedix, with one product, did not just acquire another product. It acquired an entire company with its own portfolio of products. CorMedix is transformed, for better or worse. As the deal just recently closed, please consider the inherent uncertainty associated with modeling. Below is my best attempt.

Valuation Assumptions

Now that CorMedix and Melinta are "one and the same," let’s model it (see "Appendix" for input assumptions).

My Monte Carlo simulation, which accounts for variability among key inputs, implies that while CRMD appears “overvalued” by nearly 25%, it is not unreasonably overvalued (the current price falls under my Monte Carlo's 95th percentile price of $11.45).

So, the market has higher expectations of CRMD than I do (in one scenario, 11.8% Y/Y revenue growth and 12% FCF margins result in a 0% difference between DCF value per share and current stock price).

It’s feasible that Melinta-CorMedix synergies could push FCF margins (CRMD’s primary driver) >13%, supporting CorMedix’s current valuation. However, I really feel that 10% Y/Y revenue growth and FCF margins, on average, over nine years are generous for an acute-care focused company. I believe this is a fair “base” scenario.

Final Thoughts

At ~2.7x 2025E EV/S (given the durability and quality of the sales) and a DCF of $8-9/share on conservative post-TDAPA assumptions, additional upside is challenging to envision. From my porch, TDAPA reset, DefenCath customer concentration risks, returns/stocking noise, and Melinta GTN/integration portend unfavorable risk-reward (maintain “Sell”).

Things that could change my mind include a more conservative valuation (<$10/share), CVC adoption sustained post-TDAPA, reorder cadence (returns <3% of gross), $30-45 million in synergies realized, durable hospital contribution (>$6-8 million/quarter), and post-acquisition FCF >12%.

In the near term, should DefenCath sell-in accelerate and Melinta-related synergies exceed expectations, there could be meaningful upside in CorMedix. However, unless I explicitly state otherwise, my analyses are focused on long-term fundamentals.

Appendix

DCF model input assumptions:

  • “Year 1 Revenue” of $335 million is the midpoint of management's guidance after Melinta's close.
  • While “Shares Outstanding” are closer to 78M, I estimated for 81M to allow for deal contingencies.
  • “Debt” is from Melinta’s YE‘24 notes payable.
  • “Cash” of $90 million is a pro-forma estimate due to “balance sheet” uncertainty post-acquisition.
  • “Revenue Growth” of 10% is an assumption that takes into consideration the 2025 pro-forma guide, a 2H26 headwind post-TDAPA, and modest growth thereafter.
  • “FCF Margin” of 10% is an assumption calibrated to acute-care GTN drag, returns/working capital, and the $35 million-$45 million synergy target.
  • Everything else is either standard DCF methodology (e.g., terminal growth % of 2) or was derived from financial statements (e.g., Melinta’s interest expense in 2024 implies ~9% cost of debt).

One last note: Single-point estimates can be inherently misleading. In reality, there are many combinations that can result in “DCF Value per Share = Current Stock Price.”

Per my model, CRMD’s valuation implies any combination of revenue growth (between ~9.5% and 24%) and FCF margins (between 6% and 14%).

As I always say, my primary purpose of DCF modeling is not to determine whether a stock is under- or overvalued; it is to find out what growth and margin assumptions are "baked" in. After uncovering implied assumptions, I can then evaluate whether or not they are realistic.

data.title: Palantir Stock Steadies Near $185 As AI Optimism Offsets Valuation Concerns
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya

Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR) is holding steady near $185 after rebounding from its 50-day exponential moving average (EMA), with price action continuing to respect the boundaries of its long-standing ascending channel. Despite several bouts of volatility this year, the stock has maintained its bullish structure, supported by higher lows and consistent institutional buying.

The near-term focus now centers on the $186–$188 resistance area, where a descending dotted trendline has repeatedly stalled advances. A clean breakout above this level could invite renewed momentum toward $195, with the psychological $200 threshold as the next upside target.

The broader technical structure remains favorable as long as Palantir holds above its key moving averages. Immediate support lies near $181, aligned with the 20-day EMA, while the 50- and 100-day EMAs at $176 and $169, respectively, provide deeper cushioning in the event of a correction.

The Relative Strength Index (RSI) at 59 signals a neutral-to-bullish bias, suggesting there is still room for further upside before overbought conditions emerge. A breakdown below $176, however, could shift sentiment short-term and expose the stock to a pullback toward the lower boundary of the channel near $155.

Technical momentum aligns with steady AI optimism

Palantir’s enduring rally has been fueled by sustained enthusiasm surrounding its artificial intelligence platform. The company’s unique ontology-driven data integration and decision intelligence framework have become central to its competitive moat, allowing clients across defense, energy, and enterprise sectors to adopt AI-based solutions at scale.

Recent strength in government contracts, including partnerships with NATO and the U.K. Ministry of Defense, continues to underpin revenue stability in its public sector division.

On the commercial side, Palantir’s AI operating system rollout has been gaining traction among global enterprises. Bank of America recently reaffirmed its confidence in Palantir’s business trajectory by raising its price target to $215, calling the firm one of the most strategically positioned AI companies in the U.S. tech sector. That assessment has reinforced investor conviction even as broader market momentum in AI-related equities shows signs of cooling.

Valuation remains the key restraint

Despite strong business fundamentals, Palantir’s valuation remains a flashpoint for analysts. The company trades at more than 130 times sales, the highest among S&P 500 constituents, highlighting the premium investors have placed on its long-term AI growth narrative.

Even under optimistic projections of $18 billion in annual revenue by 2030, the firm would still command a 24-times sales multiple - well above industry norms. This disconnect between robust operational execution and stretched valuation levels leaves Palantir exposed to profit-taking, particularly if sentiment toward AI equities weakens or macro headwinds intensify.

Still, the stock’s consistent technical resilience and deep institutional interest suggest that the broader uptrend remains intact. Investors appear willing to tolerate premium valuations as long as Palantir continues to deliver growth in government contracts and expand its commercial AI footprint.

Outlook

In the near term, attention will focus on whether Palantir can decisively break above the $188 resistance zone. A successful move higher could trigger a retest of $195 and eventually the $200 mark, while failure to hold above $176 would invite a deeper correction toward $155. The medium-term trend, however, remains positive, supported by ongoing AI adoption and structural demand for data-driven enterprise solutions.

Earlier analysis emphasized that Palantir’s sustained channel pattern and consistent EMA support reinforced its bullish trajectory, provided the stock held above $176. That view remains valid as price action continues to align with its long-term uptrend despite short-term valuation pressures.

data.title: Lowe's: Valuation Already Reflects Upward Trajectory
data.description:
Lowe's (NYSE:LOW) shares are still hovering around their 2021 peak, with the company still struggling to reignite its growth story after a remarkable period during the COVID-19 years.

Meanwhile, Lowe's is doing everything in its power to prepare for a market recovery that can't come soon enough. From streamlining the store fleet, exiting non-performing geographies, improving digitalization, and focusing on professional customers, Lowe's is doing all the right things.

I argue that a recovery is looming, but that Lowe's current valuation is a bit too demanding for me to go all in.

Let's dive in.

Lowe's - An American Dream

Certain businesses just feel 'American', don't they? Well, in a list that includes prominent names like Walmart (WMT), Home Depot (HD), Costco (COST), and Coca-Cola (KO), I believe that Lowe's certainly has a respected spot as well.

Founded in 1921 by Lucius Smith Low, Lowe's started as a general store in North Carolina. After his death in 1940, his son, Jim, who came back from World War 2, took over and recruited his brother-in-law, Carl Buchan, to join the company. The two of them foresaw a construction boom that would start with the war ending, and shifted the company's focus to home improvement products.

From two stores in 1949 and a valuation lower than $5 million in 1961, Lowe's grew to become the empire that it is today, with a market cap above $133 billion, 1,750 stores across the US, and nearly $84 billion in annual sales.

Lowe's has been quite the remarkable investment over the years as well, far outperforming the market from the day it went public, maintaining above-inflation revenue growth, while consistently improving margins and buying back stock, leading to double-digit EPS growth over the past 10 years.

However, the Lowe's machine has been squeaking in recent years, and newer investors can only dream about the returns and success of the past. In fact, Lowe's has been an underperformer over the past 10-year, 5-year, 3-year, and year-to-date periods.

This underperformance is primarily the result of struggles in the years that followed the COVID-19 highs.

Three Years Of Struggles Amid Higher Rates & Tough Comps

Enough has been written about the effects of the COVID-19 lockdowns with regard to Lowe's and some of its peers, so let's make this short just to provide some context.

People locked at home, receiving stimulus checks, in a zero-interest-rate environment, is the perfect recipe for record DIY activity. In other words, such a combination creates a surge in demand for Lowe's products.

Indeed, in 2020, Lowe's experienced 26.1% same-store sales growth, the highest level in decades, and 2021 was also quite the outlier with a 6.9% increase.

For retailers like Lowe's, today's strength is tomorrow's tough comparison, and we've seen the company's sales dropping sharply since those 2021 highs, with three consecutive years of negative comparable sales.

Just like COVID was the perfect mix of tailwinds for Lowe's, the last few years were exactly the opposite. Rising rates, surging inflation, and a budget-conscious consumer are making people think twice before they take on a discretionary project.

With that backdrop in mind, it's not surprising Lowe's stock is still where it was at the end of 2021, and some might argue there's a case for it being even lower.

The question is, therefore, when is the backdrop going to improve?

Lowe's Results Show Positive Signs, Even As Macro Remains Tough

Last quarter, Lowe's comparable sales rose 1.1%, the highest mark in the past couple of years. That said, comparable transactions declined 1.8%, and it was only due to a 2.9% increase in average ticket that same-store sales were up.

Lowe's discloses monthly comp performance, and it shows that July was the strongest month in the quarter, with comps up 4.7%, giving a reason to believe that Q3 will be even stronger.

Another reason to be enthusiastic emerges below the revenue line, as Lowe's delivered a strong quarter in terms of margins. Gross margin of 33.8% was the highest since 2022, while adjusted operating margins were 14.7%, up 23 bps vs. last year.

So, a 1.6% increase in revenue, combined with margin improvement and strong buybacks, has led to a 5.6% increase in EPS. Looking ahead, Lowe's upgraded its sales guidance by $1 billion and maintained its adjusted earnings outlook, which reflects mid-single-digit growth at the mid-point.

Strategically, Lowe's is making progress with its Total Home Strategy, which includes the following five pillars:

Pro penetration was up from 19% in 2019 to 30% in the first half of 2025, with an improved dedicated service, inventory optimization, and certain operational adjustments like an extended aisle and an enhanced pro app.

They've also recently closed the $8.8 billion acquisition of Foundation Building Materials, a leading distributor of interior building products serving large residential and commercial builders. The deal reflects an attractive 13x EBITDA multiple, right around Lowe's valuation but with much faster growth.

All in all, I think that it's safe to say that a) the trough is behind Lowe's, reflected by results, and b) Lowe's is becoming a stronger company amid its Total Home Strategy.

The Inevitable Home Depot Comparison

It wouldn't be a Lowe's article without a Home Depot (HD) comparison, right? There are many great articles here on Seeking Alpha that do a great job comparing the two. I can specifically point to the latest one from Dividend and Value Investor. What I'll do here is try to provide some context, instead of taking a deep dive.

I try to stick to a concentrated portfolio, and I generally refrain from the idea of investing in two companies with a very similar risk and growth profile. I believe that there's always one that is a better investment. Home Depot and Lowe's are very, very similar, just like Visa (V) and Mastercard (MA), another duo where I have a preference for one over the other, even though many just say you should invest in both.

In our case, it cannot be overlooked that Home Depot historically outperforms Lowe's in terms of growth and profitability. It also has more stores, and it still operates in Canada and Mexico, geographies that Lowe's exited, giving HD more upside in terms of footprint expansion.

And indeed, it isn't overlooked, with Home Depot trading at a 32% premium over Lowe's based on their P/E multiples. Interestingly, this is the highest premium in recent years, with the 5-year average at 25%.

Meanwhile, the way I see it, this is precisely the moment the gap should be smaller, not bigger. In fact, last quarter, Lowe's beat Home Depot on same-store sales and margins. In terms of outlook, they are both expecting about a 1% increase in same-store sales this year.

Strategically, Lowe's is making strides in the lucrative pro segment, and it's also set to deliver similar growth in upcoming years based on current consensus estimates.

So, in terms of preference, I know I would be deterred to invest in Home Depot when the premium over Lowe's is at an all-time high yet the justifications for it are the weakest they've been in years.

Homebuilders Recovery, Give Hope That Homebuilders Are Next

Current consensus estimates see Lowe's accelerating to high-single-digit EPS growth in 2026, with revenues set to accelerate to mid-single-digit growth.

This means that consensus sees Lowe's going back to its historical growth rates once the macro improves, and I think it's safe to say it's already improving, with the 10-year hovering around 4%, the beginning of a rate cut cycle, and early signs of improvement in the housing market.

In fact, from the lows of April, the Homebuilder index (XHB) gained nearly 20%, while the largest homebuilder in the country in DR Horton (DHI), is up over 35%. Meanwhile, Home Depot and Lowe's are up a much lower 10%.

Given the fact that the home improvement market shares many similar drivers with the housing market, it would make sense to believe that improvements in the latter signal improvements in the former are near.

Valuation

Even though I find the current setup more favorable to Lowe's over Home Depot, this still doesn't mean it's an attractive investment overall.

At $238 per share, Lowe's is trading at 19 times forward earnings and 18 times next year's estimates.

Notably, current valuation levels are right around historical levels, with Lowe's five-year average forward P/E at 18.

So, we're getting the historical average valuation on historical average expectations, which makes sense, and doesn't suggest a significantly attractive entry point.

In addition, a 19x P/E on 8% growth gets us to a price-to-earnings-to-growth ratio higher than 2x, generally considered expensive for a slow-growth company like Lowe's.

Taking all of that into account, I view Lowe's as a low-risk low low-reward investment here, with my price target at $255 per share by the end of 2026.

Conclusion

Lowe's is doing all the right things to prepare for an improving macroeconomic backdrop and to become a stronger business.

They are accelerating pro penetration, doing attractive and sensible acquisitions, enhancing their digital offering, and continuing to drive efficiencies in operations, reflected by the strong margins last quarter.

However, Lowe's growth trajectory remains around mid-single-digits for revenue and high-single-digits for EPS, which means a P/E of 19 gives limited upside, even though results are set to improve.

Therefore, I rate Lowe's at a 'Hold', but I wouldn't be against adding it to low-risk portfolios.

data.title: Alliance Resource Partners: Right Direction, Wrong Speed
data.description:
Introduction

Alliance Resource Partners, L.P. (NASDAQ:ARLP) (the "Company") is an energy company that primarily generates revenue from the production and marketing of coal. Over the last two decades, however, the coal industry has been in decline according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration.

Not immune to these trends, the Company had made, and continues to make, efforts to diversify its business, including (i) ownership of strategic oil & gas mineral interests in the United States and (ii) Bitcoin mining that takes advantage of the Company's stranded energy resources. I like the diversifying direction the Company is moving, but I think the Company is making two related mistakes in the Company's evolution. First, the Company recently cut its dividend by roughly 14.3%. In this regard, the CEO of the Company, Joseph Craft, noted on the most recent earnings call that

And yes, [the dividend cut] does generate the extra cash flow. And as I said, it's not because of declining fortunes. So that cash flow will be extra and it can be utilized to position ourselves for growth opportunities or pay down debt or unit buybacks or whatever makes sense for the long-term opportunities for our shareholders.

As far as do we have some immediate need to deploy the capital, there is nothing that we can announce. At the same time, we are looking at multiple areas of investment opportunities that we do think that we should pursue. So we are definitely focused on trying to grow our company and trying to have a balance sheet that we believe is strong and continues to be conservatively managed. We felt it was the proper time to, again, position ourselves for that growth."

While I agree with the rationale for the dividend cut and I am hopeful that the savings can be used for growth opportunities, the cut itself was not big enough to move the needle in my opinion (more on this below). Indeed, in my view, this was a missed opportunity and, as such, I suspect additional dividend cuts will be required in the future unless coal pricing increases materially.

Second, the Company should move more aggressively in its diversifying efforts, but it cannot do so unless there is more cash flow available to do the same. In this regard, paying out too much in dividends is hindering the Company's ability to make necessary investments in my view. For these reasons, coupled with the K-1 tax treatment, I am not inclined to acquire shares at this time. I rate them a HOLD.

This article provides a brief background of the Company, discusses its under the radar Bitcoin business, and reviews select data from the Company's June 30, 2025 financial statements.

This article will not be a deep dive on the coal business, which is highly dependent on coal pricing (i.e., like any commodity producer, the Company should soar if the price of coal soars), and for which there is plenty of information available on Seeking Alpha and other websites. Notably, coal pricing has been negative over the last 12 months for a variety of reasons, including slower growth in China. See the IEA's coal pricing analysis in its recent Coal Mid-Year Update 2025.

Brief Background

Coal

With respect to coal, per its most recent 10Q, the Company is" the second largest coal producer in the eastern United States with seven operating underground mining complexes near many of the major eastern utility generating plants and on major coal hauling railroads in Illinois, Indiana, Kentucky, Maryland, Pennsylvania, and West Virginia, as well as a coal-loading terminal in Indiana." In addition to coal operations, the Company also owns a portfolio of coal royalty investments.

Oil & Gas

In addition to coal, per its recent 10Q, the Company owns mineral interests in "approximately 70,000 net royalty acres in premier oil & gas producing regions of the United States, primarily in the Permian (Delaware and Midland), Anadarko (SCOOP/STACK), and Williston (BAkken) basins, providing [it] with diversified exposure to industry-leading operators consistent with our general strategy to grow our oil & gas mineral interest business."

Energy Infrastructure

The Company has also invested in energy and infrastructure opportunities, including investments in private companies "which are in the businesses of, respectively, electric motor manufacturing, private equity investments in renewable energy, the electrification of our economy or the efficient use of energy, and the manufacturing and recycling of sustainable, engineered battery materials for electric vehicles." See the recent 10Q linked above.

The Bitcoin Business

The Company's subsidiary, Bitiki KY, LLC houses the Company's crypto-mining activities. At December 31, 2024, the Company had 3,586 active miners and 1,232 hosted machines; its overall strategy is to monetize its underutilized electricity load (i.e., stranded energy) via bitcoin mining.

While the Company does not include the crypto-mining as its own operating segment, below I have pieced together information available in its SEC filings that sheds light on the size and scope of these operations.

At June 30, 2025, the Company had a Bitcoin portfolio worth near $60 million at a Bitcoin (BTC-USD) price of about $105,000. Moreover, for calendar year 2025, the Company is on pace to add nearly 120 more Bitcoin to its balance sheet via its bitcoin mining operations.

At its current pace, the Company could reach 1,000 Bitcoin prior to 2030, and at today's Bitcoin price of approximately $125,000, that would value the Company's Bitcoin holdings at roughly $125 million, more than double the current value on the Company's June 30, 2025 balance sheet. Of course, if Bitcoin were to rise materially over the next several years, the Company's investment in Bitcoin would increase even more.

At this juncture, even though it has been considered a pilot project, the Company's bitcoin business is on a healthy growth trajectory as shown above. The real question is whether the Company deems this business material to its future. An answer remains uncertain and the Company seems boxed in by maintaining its high dividend; indeed, shareholders of limited partnerships (like the Company) frequently expect high dividend distributions. That said, I think most of us who follow the crypto space would probably agree that had the Company announced a year ago that, in addition to mining, it was going to buy Bitcoin with a percentage of its free cash flow, the Company market cap would likely be much higher than it is today.

Selected Cash Flows Data

For me, a few things stand out. First, for the periods in question, the operating cash flow trend over the last three years is clearly going in the wrong direction.

Second, since the coal business is capital-intensive, I would expect capital expenditures to have to increase going forward, the six month figure for June 30, 2025 standing out as unusually low (particularly in light of the inflation over the last several years).

Third, because of the 14.3% dividend cut, partner distributions, which had been relatively steady, should decline going forward by about $25 million for the last six months of 2025 (or $50 million a year). Even if we back out the $25 million on a pro forma basis for the six month ended June 30, 2025, however, we see that the Net Change in Cash would still be negative to the tune of approximately $57 million for the period.

Fourth, payments on long-term debt declined materially year over year. When I look at the balance sheet, however, long-term liabilities as of June 30, 2025 are nearly the same as they were at June 30, 2024 ($822.1 million versus $829 million). Consequently, I would expect long-term payments of debt to increase materially over the last six months of 2025 (compared to the first six months of the year).

If this intuitive analysis is correct, increased debt payments and capex will not bode well for Company cash flow over the next six months, particularly when 2025 coal sales prices are largely locked in for the remainder of 2025 and most of 2026. This is why I think the dividend cut was too small.

Risks

As noted above, the coal industry has been in decline for the last two decades and coal has earned its reputation as being "dirty." As such, the industry is subject to material environmental regulation and government oversight. While the Trump administration is providing some regulatory respite for the industry, there is no guaranty that the "Red Team" will remain in charge for any material period of time. Perhaps insatiable AI energy demand will be the catalyst the industry, including the Company, needs to grow; however, natural gas, solar, nuclear and wind are likely to be the relative winners going forward. As noted above, I think future dividend cuts will be on the table unless future coal pricing improves materially. Finally, management needs to diversify the business and there are execution risks in this regard. Other risks can be found in the Company's Annual Reports (10Ks) included in the SEC Filings (linked above).

For the reasons noted above, the Company is a HOLD. Do your own due diligence.

data.title: Rates: Yields Want To Test Lower First - Let Them Overshoot
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA

The US 10yr could soon break below 4%

The US 10yr could well break below 4%, but in the medium term, we're targeting 4.25% - 4.5%. Right now, though, we're flying blind in the US as we continue to navigate the government shutdown. As a stand-alone element, it's bond positive, on the theory that a smaller pay packet for those affected means less spending. There is even an element of fear injected, as some will worry that their particular job could be gone for good (at least that's the implied threat). This is not determinative of labour market fortunes, but with a negative ADP report continuing to reverberate in the background, there is an increasingly dominant degree of apprehension about the economy.

Given that snapshot, why are long-tenor yields not falling? For a couple of reasons. First, a tariff-impacted ratchet higher in inflation in the coming months remains a risk (we think inflation heads for the 3.5% area). Second, the 10yr SOFR rate is at 3.6%. That's low versus a forward bottoming for the funds rate in the 3% area. The 10yr could, of course, compress lower, but would then be pressured to revert back up again. A 75bp curve to the funds rate should not be too much to ask for 10yr SOFR, and could be justified up at 4% given the prognosis for inflation in the coming months.

Overall, that leaves us with a justification for the 10yr Treasury yield to remain above 4%, and indeed to edge in the direction of 4.25% to 4.5% in due course. If we were to break below 4% on very poor data, such a move is justifiable given the fog out there. But it is more likely to be temporary than permanent.

It remains complicated in the eurozone

The 10yr Bund could break below 2.5%, but we're still eyeing the 3% area as an ultimate target. The straightforward stuff that argues for upside pressure on longer-dated yields centres on defence spending ambitions and associated upward pressure on fiscal deficits and supply. A more technical angle comes from the likes of Dutch pension fund reform, where the move from the regulatory straight-jacket of defined benefit to defined contribution means a forward unwinding of a large rump of 30yr to 60yr fixed rate receivers in the years ahead. There is also a bit of inflation in the eurozone. Not worrying, but enough to keep long rates on edge.

Then again, a big negative industrial production number out of Germany this week reminds us that the economic dynamo of the region remains in a state of stress. Survey evidence has seen some hints of a decent German recovery theme. But it's not enough to get overly excited by. And then there's France, where economic sentiment is going the way of Germany, with political malaise to boot. Most of the rest of the eurozone is in better shape, which helps contain spreads and wider macro risks. But there is still a macro tilt here that can absolutely worry the bond bears.

Like for US Treasuries, there is ammunition here for a break lower in the 10yr yield. But it would require quite some negativity to see it go below 2.5% for the 10yr Bund. Our baseline view remains glass half full on the economy, with the 3% area for the 10yr Bund still the medium-term target. It may take quite some months to get there, but that's the distilled view. All against a front end that's anchored by the 2% European Central Bank depo rate.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more
