data.title: Vox Royalty: Where Growth Meets Value
data.description:
All figures are in United States dollars unless otherwise noted.

Q2'25 Results

Vox Royalty (NASDAQ:VOXR) released its Q2'25 results in August, reporting quarterly revenue of ~$2.77 million, a 2% decline from the year-ago period, and operating cash flow of ~$1.76 million, a 12% decline from the year-ago period. To some, these results may appear disappointing, but it's important to note that much of this softness was related to its iron ore royalty portfolio and lower iron ore prices, overshadowing what was strong growth in royalty receipts from its gold portfolio, with a few points worth noting:

(1) Vox was lapping the cessation of mining at Koolyanobbing after a 2x payback. The result of MinRes halting production at Koolyanobbing late last year was that Vox was lapping $100,000 in revenue from this asset (Q2'24) that did not contribute this year but did in the prior-year period.

(2) The company's most significant cash flow generator, Wonmunna, contributed $1.2 million less this year relative to Q2'24. This was related to fewer tonnes of iron ore shipped in the current period vs. last year and a 10% decline in the iron ore sales price, with shipments tracking behind mining rates and lower royalty receipts largely resulting from timing, not less production.

Besides, there are three positives that the market seems to be missing.

(3) Iron ore prices have rebounded sharply to start H1'25, with iron ore prices trading materially above the Q2'25 average.

(4) We should see a catch-up in royalty receipts in H2'25 as Wonmunna shipments increase, which, combined with higher iron prices, will translate to higher revenue from this asset in H2'25.

(5) I had previously written off Koolyanobbing, with MinRes unlikely to restart this smaller asset. However, Koolyanobbing was recently sold to a private company, Yilgarn Iron. The sale price was confidential, but MinRes stated it sold to Yilgarn Iron given that "it was the buyer most likely to restart production at the complex," at this asset that has been producing since the 1960s.

Hence, it's quite possible that this asset could restart in the next couple of years, providing a boost to Vox's total revenue.

(6) Last and certainly not least, Vox just completed an extremely accretive deal that gives it considerable operating leverage and could see it increase cash flow by more than 10x in Q1'25 ($10+ million) vs. Q1'24 levels ($1.04 million).

Offtake Portfolio Acquisition

Starting with the most significant news, Vox announced the purchase of an offtake portfolio from Deterra Royalties, which was non-core to the seller, for ~$60 million ($57.5 million + $2.5 million in deferred consideration). This was financed with ~17 million shares sold at US$3.70/share with Vox Royalty's shares at new all-time highs, a refreshing instance of share dilution at favorable prices vs. many companies that leaned on their ATM or sold considerable stock in the weaker portion of the cycle in 2022-2024.

The acquisition includes 10 offtake and royalty assets across eight countries, a game-changing acquisition for a small-cap company. Notably, this deal will provide considerable operating leverage and growth in cash flow per share, with the potential for Vox to see a 10x increase in operating cash flow year-over-year in Q1'26 vs. Q1'25 ($1.1 million ---> ~$11 million) if gold remains at spot levels.

Importantly, the offtake portfolio acquisition closed on Sept. 26, meaning we will see the full benefit of this new offtake portfolio in Vox's Q4'25 results.

This incredible growth in cash flow and cash flow per share in Q1'26 is partially because of the lumpiness of the Greenstone gold offtake, which pays out its full allocation for the year in Q1 given that it's a 100% offtake up to an annual cap of 58,500 ounces. Still, the point stands that Vox will see industry-leading growth in cash flow and cash flow per share from this recent deal, and even under more conservative assumptions, Vox should generate upwards of $8.5 million in Q1'26 cash flow, translating to nearly 700% growth year-over-year.

It's also worth noting that while margins for this portfolio have seen exponential growth that has outperformed the gold price (~US$63/oz margin vs. ~$23/oz margin) from H1'22 to H1'25 or 170% growth in margins for this offtake portfolio while the gold price increased 70%, this was based on much lower gold prices in H1'25, with volatility and higher gold prices benefiting margins. Hence, it's quite possible that Vox could enjoy $80/oz+ margins in Q4'25, if not better, making for a very favorable timing for the hand-off of this portfolio from Deterra.

As for operating mines and potential restarts covered under the offtake, they include:

  • Bonikro (Cote d'Ivoire) - producing
  • Blyvoor (South Africa) - producing
  • Cove (United States) - development
  • Eagle (Canada) - for sale by receiver
  • Greenstone (Canada) - producing
  • Granite Creek (United States) - producing
  • Los Filos (Mexico) - potential restart
  • Fazenda (Brazil) - producing
  • RDM (Brazil) - producing
  • Ruby Hill (United States) - residual leach
  • Santa Luz (Brazil) - producing

Greenstone

Starting with the Greenstone Mine in Canada, this is one of Canada's largest new gold mines operated by Equinox Gold (EQX) with the offtake agreement at Greenstone being an offtake on 100% of gold production up to a capex of 58,500 ounces per year. The structure of this offtake (100% offtake, but up to 58,500 ounce cap) makes it very chunky, with it paying out primarily in Q1.

Unfortunately, the offtake agreement is only in place until March 1, 2027. Still, given that Greenstone should produce an average of 330,000+ ounces in 2026/2027, this means that Vox Royalty will see 58,500 ounces next year in Q1'26 from this offtake and upwards of 50,000 ounces in 2027, translating to ~110,000 ounces or more coming from this asset depending on the strength of Greenstone's Q1'27 results. Assuming a 1.8% margin and a $3,500/oz gold price, this translates to ~$6.9 million from this asset, assuming 110,000 ounces attributable to the offtake (up to 117,000 ounces in the upside case).

However, assuming a more similar gold price environment to the one we have today and stronger margins, which are certainly possible, we could see margins closer to $90/oz or ~$9.9 million from this single offtake alone.

Bahia Complex (Fazenda, Santa Luz) and RDM

As for other meaningful offtakes in the portfolio, Vox has acquired a multi-asset offtake on Equinox Gold's Brazilian operations (excluding Aurizona), covering the Fazenda, RDM, and Santa Luz mines. This offtake is on 35% of gold production up to a cap of 658,000 ounces, with just over 400,000 ounces delivered up to the acquisition closing date, leaving ~250,000 ounces left to Vox's account from these assets under the offtake.

These three mines should contribute upwards of 190,000 ounces combined over the next two years, translating to ~66,000 ounces to Vox per annum, making this its most significant contributor through the end of 2029 until the cap will likely be hit.

Assuming ~250,000 ounces left on the offtake and assuming a 1.8% margin at $3,500/oz gold, this translates to ~$15.8 million in contribution. However, in an upside case scenario where margins land closer to $90/oz, contribution from this offtake could come in at upwards of $22.0 million, with these two offtakes in an upside case scenario (Greenstone + Bahia Complex/RDM) already paying for ~55% of the offtake portfolio's cost (~$32 million vs. ~$57.5 million pre-deferred consideration).

Bonikro

Moving over to the Bonikro Mine in Cote d'Ivoire, this offtake agreement is highly attractive relative to the others in the portfolio, given that it's an uncapped offtake agreement covering 50% of annual gold production, meaning it could be delivering for several more years with the asset producing over 1.7 million ounces to date. And while Bonikro may have a relatively short life today based on its LOMP with reserves out to just 2029, there are a few points worth noting.

(1) 2026 is expected to be a strong Q4 and 2026 for Bonikro, according to its operator, with higher grades now exposed following a period of stripping, setting up a production year of upwards of 110,000 ounces in 2026.

(2) Allied Gold may have a short mine life at Bonikro/Agbaou, but it expects to provide an update on Oume (Dougbafla prospects) by year-end, and the company has been adamant that it's targeting a mine life extension to "over 10 years" at 180,000 to 200,000 ounces between Agbaou/Bonikro, suggesting its goal is to extend Bonikro production out past 2035.

(3) Allied Gold is investing to make this goal its reality with a $17 million increase in its original 2025 exploration budget, of which $7.5 (44%) is going to its CDI Complex (Bonikro/Agbaou).

The point here is that while ultra-conservative assumptions might suggest that the Bonikro offtake doesn't have much value with just four years of production left based on reserves (~444,000 ounces at year-end 2024), this is likely far too conservative of a view. This is especially true given that Allied Gold will be gushing cash starting in H2'26 once Kurmuk comes online, with the potential to further support aggressive exploration across the portfolio.

And if Allied Gold's goal is to become an 800,000+ ounce producer, one way to secure this future is to invest in its CDI Complex. Further, while the CDI Complex that includes Bonikro may be its smallest, it is its top-ranked jurisdiction among the African countries where it operates (Cote d'Ivoire, Ethiopia, Mali), providing benefits from a diversification and trading multiple standpoints to extending the life here aside from simply cash flow.

The other important point worth stressing is that while Bonikro is sitting on just ~444,000 ounces of total reserves, these reserves are based on what's clearly a very conservative gold price assumption of $1,800/oz. Plus, it has a considerable resource base of ~2.16 million ounces at similar grades to its reserve grades at 1.3+ G/T of gold.

Further, conversion drilling at Oume was completed earlier this year, resources have been growing rapidly here (+88% M&I growth at year-end 2024 vs. year-end 2023), and there's also an opportunity at Hire, north of Bonikro, with record gold prices certainly creating a much lower hurdle rate for mine life extension with Bonikro's reserve prices now sitting over 55% below spot gold prices.

This does not suggest that Allied Gold will increase reserve assumptions to above $3,000/oz, but there's certainly considerable wiggle room between reserve price and spot prices today vs. where the gold price started the year, with the $800/oz delta ($2,600/oz spot vs. $1,800/oz reserves) expanding to a $2,200/oz delta over the past 10 months.

Finally, Allied noted that aside from Oume, where it's looking at exploration east of Dougbafla Central and southwest of the Dougbafla West prospect, given that it has still not hit the limit of the Oume mineralized systems, it's also looking at a concept-level UG study at Hire and that this work will assess the potential for UG mining as a longer-term strategy to tap into deeper mineralization, allow for new mining fronts, and extend Bonikro's life.

For what it's worth, one opportunity previously looked at was Akissi-So, a ~2.9 million tonne UG opportunity at 3.0 G/T of gold (~280,000 ounces) during a period of much lower gold prices. And while there's no guarantee that this study gets dusted off and approved, the point is that there are multiple opportunities to unlock mine life extension, with Oume being the most likely, but others certainly available as well. In summary, I would not be shocked to see Bonikro producing past 2035, providing considerable optionality to Vox as part of its 50% offtake, given that the market doesn't appear to be giving it any credit for this.

So, what about near-term contribution?

Assuming ~115,000 in 2026 ounces and the net impact of stream ounces, we could see Bonikro contribute ~55,000 ounces to Vox's account under the offtake next year, translating to a material contribution from this asset as well. Importantly, the stream impact will reduce by Q3'26 as it hits a step-down (reducing from 6.0% to 3.5%), benefiting Vox's offtake contributions, which are net of applicable stream deliveries.

As for the total value of this offtake, it's obviously a big question mark without knowing how material mine life extension ends up being at Bonikro. However, this is assuming what I believe to be a very reasonable assumption of 500,000 ounces of future production and ~240,000 ounces to Vox's account. At a $3,500/oz gold price and 1.8% margin, this translates to ~$15.1 million in contribution to Vox from Bonikro, but this could certainly be conservative on margins and future contribution given that it assumes significantly less mine life extension than what Allied Gold is targeting.

As for an optimistic case where Bonikro produces another 700,000 ounces or over 335,000 ounces to Vox Royalty's account, contribution from this asset jumps to ~$21 million despite using conservative margin assumptions and prices well below spot levels.

Importantly, if we add the upside case for Bahia Complex/RDM (~$22 million), the upside case for Greenstone (~$10 million), and a medium case for Bonikro (~$21 million) which assumes 8 years of mine life but lower gold prices and margins, these three offtakes amount to a combined contribution of $53 million and pay for the full transaction ($57.5 + $1.25 million or ~$58.8 million).

This is because the Dandoko royalty should generate upwards of $7.0 million in contribution to Vox or $5.7 million net of fees, meaning the true cost of the offtake portfolio (net of contribution from Dandoko) is $53 million.

Granite Creek, Ruby Hill and Cove

As for the one offtake located in Nevada, Vox now holds a capped 40,000-ounce multi-asset offtake on the Granite Creek, Ruby Hill and Cove assets until December 2028. Aside from Cove, which is unlikely to contribute in the offtake period (requires permits and dewatering), Granite Creek UG is already producing at a rate of 40,000 ounces per annum as it ramps up to higher mining rates, and Ruby Hill is set to kick in by late 2026 (aside from a minimal contribution from residual leaching, given that its Archimedes UG Mine is now approved for development above the 5,100 foot level.

Unfortunately, this is a capped offtake until December 2028, but I would expect the full 40,000 ounces to be delivered under each year of the offtake, with Granite Creek UG likely to produce upwards of 40,000 ounces next year, with Granite Creek UG and Ruby Hill set to see a steady ramp-up to a combined 80,000 ounces in 2027 (Archimedes UG will start contributing meaningfully by H2'27) and upwards of 150,000 ounces combined from these two assets in 2028.

Hence, Vox should see deliveries from this offtake agreement of 120,000+ ounces (40,000 ounces per year in 2026, 2027, 2028) plus a minor contribution in Q4'25, translating to a near $8.0 million contribution using conservative margins and a $3,500/oz gold price or upwards of $11.0 million in an upside case with margins closer to $90/oz.

In summary, and while capped, this Nevada multi-asset offtake is another meaningful contributor in a short period, with Vox Royalty taking hold of this multi-asset offtake just as production starts ramping up to ensure deliveries in line with the annual cap vs. lower production years previously.

Blyvoor

Last but not least among producing assets, Vox has a 100% gold offtake (net of stream ounces) on the Blyvoor Mine in South Africa owned by Aurous. This asset has less disclosure and is a smaller asset today from a production profile standpoint but has a reserve base (as of Q1'24) of ~5.14 million ounces at 5.54 G/T of gold on a 100% basis. If optimized, this asset has the potential to see annual production grow to ~148,000 ounces/year on average in the 2027-2036 period. Currently, the company is hoping to list on the Nasdaq via a business combination with Rigel Resource Acquisition Corp. with a targeted PIPE raise of $50 million, backed by Orion Resource Partners.

Even under more conservative assumptions (25% fewer ounces than projected, net of stream deliveries), this targeted production growth, if achieved, would translate to more than 100,000 ounces delivered to Vox per annum under its offtake for 20-plus years into the 2050s before the cap is hit. As for contribution to Vox and even using more conservative long-term gold prices and margins ($3,000/oz, 1.6% margin) given that this asset could take a few years to start ramping up toward the 100,000+ ounce mark where contributions are more meaningful, Vox could see a contribution of nearly $5.0 million/year, making this another meaningful contributor, assuming it gets the capital it needs and is successfully optimized.

Putting it all together and using a 6% discount rate for the offtake portfolio (ex-Blyvoor), a 10% discount rate for Blyvoor, more conservative production assumptions on Blyvoor, and a $3,500/oz gold price in 2026 through 2028 at a 1.7% margin ($59.50/oz) and a $3,000/oz gold price at a 1.40% margin post-2028 ($42.00/oz), the estimated NAV for this offtake portfolio comes in at ~$77 million, an incredible value for the price paid of ~$53 million (*), translating to a very attractive multiple of ~0.69x NAV.

However, I would argue that this NAV estimate of ~$77 million severely understates the potential cash flow from this portfolio given that it uses conservative long-term gold price assumptions and very conservative margins when there's a real possibility that margins average upwards of $80/oz in 2026 and 2027. Still, even under these conservative assumptions, given that it's hard to forecast where the gold price trades medium-term and its level of volatility, this was a clear home-run transaction for Vox.

(*) $53 million purchase price is based on ~$58.8 million acquisition cost ($57.5 million + $1.25 million deferred consideration [-] expected contributions from Dandoko royalty). I have not counted the other $1.25 million in deferred consideration, given that Dandoko's current reserves do not support a 500,000 ounce production threshold being hit, making the effective offtake/royalty portfolio price ~$58.8 million, not $60.0 million (*).

So, what do the contributions look like?

As we can see, contributions from this offtake portfolio should average closer to 250,000 ounces over the next two years before dipping in 2028 with Greenstone hitting its cap (2027), offset by the planned restart of production at the Sugar Zone in Canada and higher production at Blyvoor. Deliveries are then expected to trend lower with a higher weighting to Blyvoor post-2029 as the Brazilian multi-asset offtake hits its cap, but as noted previously, Bonikro could easily be extended past 2033, and while it's a steep drop-off in production post-2028, the cost of this portfolio (~$53 million) will largely have been paid off from cash flow in these three years as well as Q4'25, with any upside post-2028 being gravy to Vox Royalty.

The enormous piece of the puzzle that the market seems to be missing, though, is that:

(1) Higher for longer gold prices could accelerate the payback of this offtake portfolio and drive returns much higher, with the potential for upwards of $20 million in cash flow per year in peak years.

(2) The above conceptual offtake contributions do not include any contributions from Los Filos, a major potential contributor if restarted, nor do they include Allied delivering on its mine life extension goals at Bonikro (2035+).

(3) The major wild card is Eagle, where the future of the offtake contract and a restart is uncertain, but in a best-case scenario, this would be another massive contributor.

Importantly, and as the upside case outlook below shows, Los Filos and Eagle could pick up all the slack from the Nevada portfolio and Greenstone rolling off in late 2028 and early 2027, respectively, and would drive offtake contributions well above 300,000 ounces in peak years with a much more gradual decline in offtake contributions. Obviously, there are no guarantees that Los Filos is restarted, the Eagle offtake remains in place and/or either restart this decade.

However, with the potential for Los Filos to pursue its expansion with two communities and/or move into the hands of a different owner that might be willing to settle at terms requested by Carrizalillo's terms, I don't think a restart at Los Filos is far-fetched, and with Vox owning 50% offtake with nearly 600,000 ounces left, this could be a huge contributor even using more conservative estimates than the 2022 FS, given that any restart may rely on less ground than initially contemplated under the 2022 FS.

In fact, even assuming an average production rate of 250,000 ounces vs. the 360,000 ounces in its best years envisioned in the 2022 FS, this would translate to ~125,000 ounces/year in offtake contributions to Vox for multiple years, translating to over $6 million/year in contribution even using very conservative margin assumptions.

As for the Eagle Mine, even assuming a more conservative 180,000 ounces of production per year on average in the first five years post-restart vs. closer to 205,000 ounces in the LOMP, Vox's 25% offtake would translate to ~45,000 ounces to its account per year for several years until the cap is hit, which conveniently still has over 900,000 ounces left until the cap is reached, meaning decades of potential offtake contribution at a ~40,000 ounce annual contribution.

Obviously, there are no guarantees Vox will see deliveries from either asset, especially at Eagle, given that it requires a restart after a heap-leach pad failure and the survival of the offtake contract here. However, in the best-case scenario that these assets restart, this deal will look even more genius given that Eagle and Los Filos can combine for 160,000-plus ounces/year in contributions, doubling the long-term profile of this offtake portfolio and materially de-risking it with less weight to Blyvoor and more weight to North American assets.

Royalties (Included in Recent Acquisition)

Finally, I'd be remiss not to point out that while Vox has transformed itself with this offtake portfolio acquisition, it also added two royalties in the process, increasing to more than 70 royalties across its portfolio. The royalties include an effective 1.04% GRR at St. Ives covering mining lease M15/495, which hosts the Incredible OP, where a small resource was reported as of its 2021 TR that included ~39,000 ounces at 1.34 G/T of gold.

This is not a near-term producing asset, but it adds a Tier-1 jurisdiction royalty (Australia) on a flagship operation owned by a top-five gold producer by scale in Gold Fields (GFI), especially when it comes at no cost given that the offtake portfolio was already bought at a huge discount. Hence, this new royalty is certainly a nice-to-have addition to the portfolio for future discovery optionality.

In addition to the St. Ives royalty is the much closer to cash-flowing 1.0% NSR on Dandoko (subject to a $1.25 million payment), with Dandoko lying east of the 400,000+ ounce Fekola Mine (Fekola OP, Fekola UG, Cardinal) as part of the larger Fekola Complex. For those unfamiliar, Dandoko was scooped up in the Oklo Resources transaction, it's part of Fekola Regional (southeast of Anaconda), and it hosts a reserve base of ~230,000 ounces at 3.22 G/T of gold (~2.2 million tonnes), and a total resource base of ~444,000 ounces.

Importantly, this is already in the mine plan at the Fekola Complex, with ore from Dandoko expected to be trucked ~30 kilometers west to the Fekola Mill, with three pits expected to be mined to a maximum depth of 140 meters at the Seko 2 pit. The expectation is that Dandoko will contribute ~65,000 ounces on average per annum (peak production of 75,000 ounces) in the 2028 to 2032 period, with mining set to begin in 2027. At a ~65,000-ounce production profile, a $3,500/oz gold price, and Vox's 1.0% NSR, Dandoko will contribute more than $2.2 million in revenue per annum over its short life, making this a solid little asset for Vox later this decade with a similar annual contribution to Binduli North (Janet Ivy).

Red Hill Royalty

Moving over to Red Hill, which is Vox's flagship royalty asset (4.0% GRR on M27/57 that is estimated to cover upwards of 85% of reserves or an effective 3.5% GRR) that it secured for ~$5.0 million, it now boasts an estimated NAV of ~$80 million ($3,500/oz gold price). Further, Northern Star (OTCPK:NESRF) recently released its year-end reserves/resources update, with some updated information on Red Hill, which, for those unfamiliar, is a near-surface and low-strip gold deposit just north of its massive Fimiston plant that's currently seeing its capacity doubled to 27 million tonnes per annum, roughly the same size as Agnico Eagle's (AEM) Detour Lake plant.

For those unfamiliar, the beauty of Red Hill is that it is low-capex, very quick to put into production, and can displace low-grade stockpiles, potentially helping to push KCGM's annual production closer to 1.0 million ounces per year vs. its stated goal of 900,000 ounces per year post-2028. As we can see below, Red Hill lies just east of Northern Star's Kanowna Belle UG operations and a short haul from KCGM (further south), making this a very attractive satellite opportunity. And given this strategic location and ability to feed higher grades to its expanded plant, Northern Star hasn't been shy highlighting this opportunity in past presentations.

Looking at recent disclosures from Northern Star, we can see that mineralization has clearly expanded from the right image (March 2024) to the left image (March 2025), and Red Hill has delivered more impressive intercepts. These include 98.9 meters at 1.6 G/T of gold at the base of the planned reserve pit, 87 meters at 2.3 G/T of gold within the reserve pit at shallower depths, 113 meters at 2.1 G/T of gold, and 91.1 meters at 2.1 G/T of gold outside of the reserve pit.

As for deeper hits, Red Hill hit a very thick intercept of 308.5 meters at 1.3 G/T of gold as well as 97.5 meters at 1.5 G/T of gold and 103.7 meters at 1.6 G/T of gold outside of its current resource shell, highlighting significant potential to expand this resource further.

As for resource figures, while Northern Star didn't give an exact figure for Red Hill this year, it shared that Kanowna surface resources had increased to ~78.5 million tonnes at 1.3 G/T of gold for ~3.18 million ounces of gold. It also provided a maiden resource figure of ~13.4 million tonnes at 2.1 G/T of gold for Hercules for ~905,000 ounces, a satellite opportunity south of KCGM.

So if we back out the ~905,000 ounces at Hercules, we come up with a ~2.28 million ounce resource at Red Hill, or roughly ~65 million tonnes at 1.09 G/T of gold. As we can see, this is a further step up in resource growth, with the Red Hill resource nearly doubling from 2023 levels (~1.2 million ounces). As for the potential contribution to Vox, which could start as early as late 2027, and assuming an annual throughput of 4.0 MTPA, grades closer to 1.1 G/T of gold, an 88% recovery rate + a 3.4% effective royalty rate, Red Hill could produce ~125,000 ounces per year (potentially higher in peak years), translating to ~4,300 ounces per annum to Vox. At spot gold prices ($3,900/oz), this translates to ~$17 million/year to Vox, making this an extremely valuable royalty asset.

That said, the asset has seen some negative press recently, with litigation where Vox has been named as the second defendant, with the plaintiff noting that it had a right of first refusal on the Red Hill royalty sale, which was part of a portfolio of Australian royalties sold in 2023. Vox has stated the following:

"Vox's position is that the purported right of first refusal did not apply in the circumstances, and will take all appropriate steps to defend the action."

Obviously, the outcome in one case disputing a ROFR on an Australian gold royalty does not decide the outcome of an unrelated case where Grange and have been named first and second defendants, respectively. Still, I do not see this as a deal-breaker, and I'm cautiously optimistic regarding this dispute. Further, I believe that a positive resolution could allow the stock to see a sharp catch-up vs. peers.

However, it's important to note that aside from this temporary uncertainty, we continue to see overwhelmingly positive results from the company, solidifying the investment thesis here even in an ex-Red Hill scenario. Let's take a look at some recent developments:

Recent Developments

Balance Sheet

As for Vox's balance sheet and liquidity, the company reduced its debt to under $7.0 million in Q3 after a $5 million repayment on its RCF balance of $11.7 million, and Vox will exit 2025 back in a net cash position despite coming off a massive transaction, given that it was funded with minimal dilution using strong currency at all-time highs. As for other benefits, this deal has transformed the company and opened it up to larger index inclusion with its higher weighting to gold, such as the Gold Juniors Index (GDXJ).

The company also upsized its credit facility to $40 million with a $35 million accordion, giving it significant capacity to execute new deals that might come across the plate, with over $80 million in liquidity.

Bullabulling

As for the Bullabulling Project acquired by Minerals260, the team has been beefing up its executive management with a new COO that was previously General Manger at Oz Minerals Carrapateena copper-gold mine as well as Head of Technical Excellence at OZ Minerals and Advisor to the CEO. Prior to this, Jack Dermody (incoming COO at Minerals 260) was Mining Operations Manager at Cadia (Newcrest), a massive copper-gold mine now owned by Newmont (NEM) and UG production manager/superintendent prior to this role. This is a significant upgrade to Minerals 260's leadership and certainly improves the outlook for this junior that is looking to bring Bullabulling online by H2-28.

In terms of this asset's importance to Vox, it holds a A$10/oz or ~US$6.50/oz royalty on Bullabulling once 75,000 additional ounces have been produced to reach the hurdle rate. Assuming a ~190,000-ounce production profile and partial coverage on this royalty, this should translate to nearly $0.8 million in revenue, with Vox's royalty covering the Dicksons and Phoenix pits that make up 50% of the current ~2.3 million-ounce resource base.

Notably, the Bullabulling production royalty (uncapped) is yet another case of buying a dollar for 35 cents or less, with Vox paying total consideration (upfront/contingent) of ~$1.4 million for an asset that, assuming at least ~700,000 ounces are produced from Phoenix/Dicksons (60% conversion of current total resources), would translate to up to $4.5 million in total revenue coming to Vox, with potential for higher contribution longer-term given that these pits can certainly be deepened.

As for production, Bullabulling was previously expected to average ~650,000 ounces in the first three years, suggesting contribution to Vox l could be closer to $1.0 million in revenue per year in peak years, with the potential for this asset to contribute by mid-2029 once the hurdle is passed. Digging into recent drilling results, MI260 recently reported solid intercepts of 6.15 meters at 2.71 G/T of gold from 36 meters, seven meters at 2.6 G/T of gold from 29 meters and three meters at 13.3 G/T of gold from 293 meters. Notably, the bulk of historical drilling (530,000 meters) was down to a depth of ~50 meters and deeper drilling certainly shows that mineralization continues at depth at attractive grades, either below or on the edge of the A$3,000/oz planned pits.

Finally, drilling previous to the most recent release, MI260 hit 62 meters at 1.1 G/T of gold on the bottom of the resource pit and as for drilling at Dicksons (also royalty-covered), intercepts include 12 meters at 1.8 G/T of gold, also on the bottom of the resource pit, suggesting upside to this resource on royalty-linked ground and for the total 8+ kilometer strike length that covers the main deposits. In terms of next steps, a resource update is expected by year-end that should see a further increase in the resource vs. the historical resource, with the company using current drilling to inform its pre-feasibility and feasibility studies next year, with a targeted Final Investment Decision by year-end 2026.

Lynn Lake

Moving over to Lynn Lake, Alamos has seen a slow start to construction, and first production is now slated for H2'28 which is out of the company's control because of wildfires in Manitoba. However, once in production and the initial capex is recovered, mining on the MacLellan deposit will benefit Vox for its 2.0% gross proceeds royalty, with coverage on the western portion of the pit. On a positive note, initial capex will be recovered much quicker in a $3,000/oz plus gold price environment to accelerate the start of royalty receipts from this asset. Further, this is another example of shrewd investing, with the portfolio below purchased for just $800,000 with eight royalties (six were kept as two had ROFRs).

With Alamos approving Lynn Lake, royalties from Lynn Lake alone will more than cover the purchase price while the company gets free long-term exposure to:

1) Montanore (Libby) - a massive copper project in Montana

2) Estrades - a polymetallic project in Quebec

3) Kenbridge, a low capex nickel project in Ontario

4) royalty ground surrounding the Maclellan deposit at Lynn Lake, including a nickel royalty

5) a royalty on land lying between Agnico Eagle's LaRonde and Malartic mines, two of the largest assets in its portfolio

Obviously, there's no guarantee that any of these other five assets will pay out over the next decade, but as Vox has done on multiple occasions, it picks up royalty portfolios where a single asset in the portfolio covers the purchase price and the rest become long-term optionality at no cost to shareholders.

South Railroad (South Carlin Complex)

As for Vox's royalty on the South Carlin Project in Nevada, we recently saw the operator receive its Notice of Intent, a key milestone in the permitting process, and now it expects its Record of Decision in Q3-26, allowing for first production in early 2028, and it shared that it could begin procurement by year-end. As Vox investors may be aware, the company has ~$115,000 in annual advance payments related to this royalty, as well as a 0.633% NSR on several deposits, including Jasperoid Wash, Bald Mountain, as well as coverage on the bulk of Pinion and the south portion of the Main Dark Star pit.

Notably, Vox's royalty also covers Pinion SB, the southeast extension of Pinion, and the more recently drilled Jasperoid Wash extension, as well as partial coverage of other targets on the large southern gold soil anomaly. Given where Vox's royalty coverage lies on the two deposits that make up the mine plan (Dark Star, Pinion), we may not see meaningful contributions from this asset until 2031, but this should contribute upwards of ~$1.5 million per year in some of its best years while mining is focused at Pinion, where Vox holds the most significant coverage.

However, having exposure to multiple deposits and the second largest land package on the Carlin Trend is a great position to be in, especially with an operator that drills more aggressively than peers and is not going to settle on just an ~8 year mine life at South Carlin. In fact, it would ideally expand operations and bring new deposits and extensions into the mine plan, assuming resource growth justifies an expansion and/or mine life extension. This is important because Vox has full coverage of Pinion SB, which, besides depth extensions at Pinion, is likely the best opportunity for mine-life extension at South Railroad, being firmly on Vox's ground.

Kanmantoo

Moving to its Kanmantoo copper-gold royalty in Australia, this was another exceptional transaction by the company, paying $11.7 million for a 2.5% NSR (stepdown to 0.5% after cumulative production of 85,000 tonnes of copper, with ~70,000 tonnes left), paying just ~3x forward revenue for a cash-flowing asset in a top-ranked jurisdiction.

And while royalties with major step-downs are often penalized, the most important takeaway is that this production profile (~14,000 tonnes of copper, ~5,000 ounces of gold plus additional silver) is based on a ~1.4 million tonne per annum mining rate, with an installed capacity at its plant of 3.6 million tonnes per annum and peak throughput in 2015 of ~4.1 million tonnes per annum.

Hence, this suggests that this asset is capable of much more than what it's producing today if it can develop new mining fronts and increase mine production to fill the plant. This is a big deal because the step-down on this royalty that will impact cash flow generation to Vox by the end of this decade is not nearly as meaningful if the operator can more than double throughput.

As an illustration, if we see ~1.8 million tonnes processed or ~380 copper tonnes to Vox's account in 2026, but throughput doubles to ~3.6 MTPA in 2030 when the step-down is reached (0.5% vs. 2.5%), Vox will still receive royalty receipts attributable to ~150 tonnes, or half or 2/5 of its prior contribution on an attributable basis, despite an 80% decline in royalty rate. In my view, the ability to operate at 3.0+ MTPA later this decade is not a stretch at all with a swarm of targets within 2 kilometers of underground development both north and south of the past-producing Giant Pit and drilling success everywhere the company points its drills to date.

As for recent production, some investors might have been less elated with the dip in sequential production, but it's important to note that this was related to high-grade tonnes being deferred to Q3 and not an issue with grade reconciliation. In fact, the operator confirmed in its Q2'25 conference call that grades were already trending back towards reserve grades with a better Q3'25 expected.

The more exciting news, though, is that Nugent development (next mining front) has been accelerated and first stope production is on track for this quarter. This is a big deal because it lifts throughput to up to 1.8 million tonnes per annum (half of installed capacity), setting Kanmantoo up to produce upwards of 15,000 tonnes of copper next year, translating to increased royalty receipts to Vox. Just as exciting is the fact that drill results out of Emily Star were exceptional, including a thick intercept of ~19.1 meters at 1.91% copper and 0.15 G/T of gold, well above reserve grades.

Further, and just one week prior to the Emily Star hits being released, Kanmantoo's operator delivered more high-grade copper hits at Nugent, with Hillgrove stating the following:

Recent highlight hits at Nugent included 21 meters at 0.85% copper and 0.98 G/T of gold and 11.5 meters at 0.99% copper and 0.79 G/T of gold, well above the gold grades estimated in its most recent exploration target of 0.7% - 1.3% copper and 0.3% - 0.5% gold.

While the growth at Nugent is exciting, as is the fact that Hillgrove raised ~$18 million to ramp up drilling and development at Emily Star, which lies directly south of Nugent, is the fact that Emily Star provides the ability to add incremental tonnes and get closer to filling the Kanmantoo plant, which remains severely under-utilized. And while all of the recent focus has been on the transformational offtake portfolio, it's encouraging to see the Kanmantoo royalty looking better each month, which was Vox's most significant transaction this year leading up to the offtake portfolio acquisition.

As for the big picture standpoint and assuming Hillgrove can ramp to even 2.5 million tonnes per annum at 0.90% copper by early 2028, we could see copper production increase to ~21,000 tonnes of copper or ~$5.0 million in annual revenue to Vox until its royalty step-down from 2.5% to 0.5% is hit.

Permitting Timelines (Australia)

While not related to any specific assets, one potentially overlooked part of the Vox thesis is that while Vox has the bulk of its NAV tied to Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions like its NAV distribution favors ground in Australia vs. Canada, This is very important in terms of time to cash flows and while Western Australia, Nevada, Ontario and Quebec are all prolific jurisdictions for mineral endowment and geologic potential, permitting timelines can be multiples faster in Western Australia.

This was evidenced by disclosure from MINEDEX that the Janet Ivy expansion was submitted on May 1st, 2025, and approved on July 16th, a 10-week turnaround for a major expansion on a brownfields asset that added 2 million tonnes/year of excess capacity, which is not an insignificant figure by any means, translating to an additional ~$0.65 million/year to Vox at this per-tonne royalty.

Overall, this bodes well for Red Hill (past producer on a producing mining complex), which is also a simple satellite opportunity and could be permitted very quickly (potentially in 3 months or less). The favorable timelines are also positive news for the rest of Vox's Australian development portfolio, where operators are benefiting from record gold prices, better access to capital, and the ability to put restarts into motion very quickly.

Wyloo North Iron Ore Royalty

As for other recent deals, Vox completed another incredible transaction in the latter portion of Q3, scooping up a 1.5% gross revenue on an iron-ore royalty held by a major operator (Fortescue) in Australia that is likely a few years away from first production. The royalty does have a cap that triggers after the first 15,000,000 tonnes of iron ore production. That said, even at $90/tonne iron ore prices, 15MM tonnes of iron ore carries a value of US$13.5 million, which at a 1.5% royalty is over US$20 million in revenue attributable to Vox.

Deals like these may seem insignificant from a purchase price standpoint, but when you're consistently buying $1.00 in value or more for a quarter or less (potentially a dime or less in this case), these are exciting deals to beef up and further diversify the portfolio. Further, it's nice to see the company continuing to be counter-cyclical and take advantage of great royalty assets in weak periods of commodity pricing, similar to Franco-Nevada's (FNV) model, where its primary focus is gold/silver, but it's more than willing to venture into other commodities in periods of softness when the asset and price are right.

Valuation

Based on ~71 million fully diluted shares and a share price of US$4.15, Vox Royalty trades at a market cap of ~$295 million and an enterprise value of ~$297 million. This leaves the company trading at a massive discount to other junior royalty peers like Metalla Royalty (MTA) at a market cap of ~$670 million, and this is despite the fact that Vox Royalty is set to generate twice as much cash flow next year following its transformational offtake royalty portfolio acquisition.

However, 2026 cash flow generation does not do the company justice relative to the long-term value of this portfolio with major contributors that could head into production like Bowdens (largest silver project in Australia), Red Hill (major satellite deposit north of KCGM), South Carlin (high-grade oxide project sitting on the second largest land package on the Carlin Trend), and Sulphur Springs (a VMS deposit in Australia with Bill Beament at the helm of the company).

Further, Vox Royalty has sleepers in its portfolio, like a 1.0% GRR on Limpopo (~36 million ounces of platinum group metal resources) on a site with an existing shaft and concentrator, with a 1.0% GRR on Dwaalkop and a 0.704% GRR on the Voorspoed and Doornvlei title areas (shown below). It also holds a per tonne royalty on Libby, a massive copper project in Montana with resources of ~180 million ounces of silver and over 700,000 tonnes of copper, as discussed previously. These could have a material impact on its royalty receipts if approved, but for conservatism I am not counting on them coming into production this decade.

However, perhaps the most meaningful piece of the Vox Royalty investment thesis overlooked by the market is its proprietary database of 8,500+ royalties focused primarily in Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions, giving Vox a near never-ending stream of potential new acquisitions without the worry about getting into bidding wars on assets.

And while the average royalty/streaming company with this database might be levered to the gills and chasing growth at any cost to inflate their GEO sales given the growth-for-growth’s sake mentality that dominates this sector, Vox has consistently maintained a very clean balance sheet, extreme discipline regarding price paid when transacting, and a focus on the best jurisdictions for its royalty portfolio to protect every dollar going out the door.

In fact, there are few companies in the mid-tier and large-cap space as disciplined as Vox (let alone the micro-cap space) when it comes to acquisitions. I see this as a huge differentiator for Vox Royalty, with it not only having a clear competitive advantage but also the discipline to use this asset (royalty database) selectively with a rigid focus on risk-adjusted returns. For this reason, it’s difficult (in a good way) to model Vox’s growth because this is a company that can deploy a relatively small sum like $20 million and potentially add $6 - 10 million in annual cash flow 18 months out on high-margin royalties, a figure that its peers cannot reproduce.

Today, all eyes are on Red Hill. And while the situation is understandably weighing on the stock’s sentiment given that this asset carries an estimated NAV of $80+ million at spot prices, it’s important to remember that this dispute does not affect current cash flows, the company already has a significant royalty/offtake business excluding Red Hill that should generate well over $30 million in revenue next year. Plus, it has over 70 other royalties in this portfolio (and several that are already producing) separate from Red Hill.

The uncertainty around Red Hill does not affect the company's ability to self-fund acquisitions through cash flows, it will not take a bite out of its current financial results, and it’s not a thesis-breaker like it might be for an less diversified royalty company.

So, what's a fair value for the stock?

Using what I believe to be conservative multiples of 1.1x P/NAV (6%) and 16x FY2026 cash flow estimates using conservative gold price assumptions and a 65/35 weighting to P/NAV vs. P/CF for a Tier-1 jurisdiction-focused royalty company, I see an updated fair value for VOXR of US$5.35/share (29% upside from current levels). Using less conservative gold price assumptions, albeit still below spot levels ($3,900/oz in 2026), Vox's fair estimated value jumps to US$6.00/share.

However, assuming Red Hill comes online with a full year of production in 2028 as well as contributions from its offtake portfolio, Vox has the potential to generate upwards of $38 million in cash flow in 2028, assuming a $3,500/oz gold price. In my view, a company with a diversified royalty portfolio and a chunky cornerstone royalty asset at a top-5 mine by scale globally (KCGM) should trade at a minimum of 16x free cash flow, and even 18x free cash flow would not be a stretch, placing Vox Royalty's longer-term fair value at US$610 million to $685 million, or US$8.50/share to US$9.50/share on ~72 million fully diluted shares.

Importantly, this assumes that Vox makes no major acquisitions with its growing cash balance, which could be deployed into new royalty acquisitions, potentially beefing up its cash flow profile even further. However, assuming we see no new acquisitions and a flat $3,500/oz gold price, the company would exit 2028 with ~$90 million in net cash or US$1.25/share. Hence, Vox Royalty is truly a free cash flow machine hiding in plain sight.

Finally, while I prefer to focus on the fundamentals, Vox Royalty has one of the more attractive technical setups sector-wide. This is because, unlike some moose pasture micro-caps that have blasted into the stratosphere on weak fundamental foundations, Vox Royalty is a real business with a smart and hungry management team that's generating meaningful free cash flow and has only recently pushed to new all-time highs out of a multi-year base.

Summary

In a sector where there is far too much sloppy share dilution vs. smart share dilution, Vox Royalty has recently completed one of the most accretive deals in the past few years, punching above its weight to acquire a major offtake portfolio at a dirt-cheap valuation that offers considerable optionality. Further, Vox has demonstrated an impressive ability to consistently transact at industry-leading IRRs and looks to be proving that it can deploy capital at these superior rates with scale. Further, even when not deploying large sums of capital in smaller deals, it's generating enormous value (Wyloo North).

With the stock hitting new all-time highs, some investors might believe that it's too late to be adding exposure. However, a simple look at where other junior royalty companies trade is evidence that Vox is still deeply undervalued, trading at a deep discount to peers.

As for catalysts, there is a lot to look forward to across Vox's portfolio aside from what will be transformational Q4'25 and Q1'26 results, with Vox enjoying considerable operating leverage. This includes updated resources at Bullabulling, updated resources at Kanmantoo, updated resources and economic studies at Bonikro and South Carlin, and the start of production from three new Australian gold royalties in Q4’25 and into next year (Mt. Ida, Bruno Lewis, Horseshoe Lights).

To summarize, I see Vox Royalty as one of the most attractive names in the small-cap gold space, and I would view any weakness in the stock as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Model Portfolio For Income, October 2025
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The following model portfolio was designed for investors seeking income, either building a portfolio for retirement or withdrawing income immediately. It uses the best ideas of BDCs, MLPs, CEFs, Option Income funds and REITS that I currently invest in to produce an annual income.

Within the portfolio, some investments pay out a consistent monthly or quarterly income, and some are chosen to grow the dividend over time. The result is an income flow that can be withdrawn monthly and will continue to grow adjusting for inflation. Assume investing the same amount (4%) in each security. For a $100,000 portfolio, that’s $4,000 in each position. For a higher conviction portfolio, choose 20 investments.

The securities that I cover here will have the following criteria in common:

  • Top-tier investment companies with sophisticated research and knowledge.
  • 25 securities with a weighting of 4% each to ensure diversification, avoid idiosyncratic risk.
  • A mix of quarterly and monthly distributions, assuming an investor will want to withdraw monthly.
  • Proven investments with history, unlikely to surprise investors.
  • Liquidity, easy to trade, given size and volume of trading.
  • Have ratings of Hold to Strong Buy with the majority of analysts.
  • Focused on income as a priority.
  • Many of the securities are tax efficient but it is not the focus.
  • Total return is important in selecting quality securities that will last over time.

Full disclosure, I own every security mentioned in this article and am continually researching the best ideas. In some cases, I’ve owned them for years, understanding the pros and cons. That also helps me drop some ideas in the current environment and highlight others that are ideal under the circumstances. Many of the selections were introduced to me through Seeking Alpha by like-minded investors trying to achieve a level of income for retirement or building toward it.

Model Portfolio

The chart below shows the model portfolio for income. The columns include:

  1. Sym – Symbol.
  2. Type – Business Development Company (BDC), Closed End Fund. (CEF), Master Limited Partnership (MLP), Real Estate Investment Trust (REIT), Exchange-Traded Fund (ETF).
  3. Yld Fw – Forward Dividend Yield. (In some cases the trailing 12 month yield will be more useful, so I recommend looking at both and the full history of payouts).
  4. Div. G3 – Dividend Growth Rate over 3 Years.
  5. Freq. – Frequency of distribution, either monthly or quarterly
  6. 5 or 10 Yr. TR – 5-year Total Return (Price appreciation plus dividends).

Portfolio Names and Rationale to Own

The model portfolio can be divided into diversified sources of income. The same way a single stock can add idiosyncratic risk, overreliance on one source of income can present risks if the sector is affected negatively by macroeconomic forces. This portfolio can be broken down further into REITs, BDCs, bond funds, MLPs, dividend income funds, and derivative or option income funds.

Business Development Companies - BDCs

BDCs lend money to small and middle market businesses, that typically can’t borrow from a large bank. There are many analysts on Seeking Alpha writing about BDCs. The group below are the names that stand out as best of breed.

Ares Capital Corp. (ARCC) – one of the largest and widely held BDCs, Ares pays a quarterly dividend yielding 9.76%, with a 10-year total return of 229%. The rate is lower than some other BDCs because it is more conservative in its lending standards, therefore lower risk. This could be considered a core holding of any income portfolio.

FIDUS Investment (FDUS) – pays a quarterly dividend with a 11.35% yield. Within BDC’s it occupies a unique position of leadership, in my view. It has a 10-year total return of 315%, the highest in the portfolio. This unique company has expertise in the space but isn’t a household name, so its often overlooked.

Kayne Anderson BDC, Inc. (KBDC) - is a relatively new first lien BDC, with the least leveraged balance sheet in the sector. It pays a quarterly distribution with 11.79% yield. Many analysts on Seeking Alpha are now preferring it over ARCC.

Bond Funds

I’ve included multiple funds for this portfolio, all run by expert operators. They all pay monthly distributions, providing a portion of stability to the income payments.

Ares Dynamic Credit Allocation Fund, Inc. (ARDC) – holds a combination of loans, bonds, collateralized loan obligation - CLO debt, and CLO equity. It pays a monthly dividend yielding 9.81% annually, with an 72% 5-year total return. Ares is a leading alternative asset management company.

PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund (PDO) – at just over three years old, the fund pays a monthly distribution yielding 10.82%. Current income is its primary objective, which is realized by utilizing a dynamic asset allocation strategy.

The Pacific Investment Management Company - PIMCO, pioneered bond trading under Bill Gross. The company is still the gold standard of bond funds. I personally prefer the closed end funds such as PDO because their distribution yields are much larger than a corporate bond ETF yielding 5%.

As an incentive for reinvesting dividends, PIMCO offers a 5% discount on the reinvested purchase price. I reinvest PDO dividends in my Fidelity account and can confirm the discount applies.

PIMCO Income Strategy Fund II (PFN) – like PDO, the fund has a monthly distribution yielding 11.28%, with the same reinvestment incentive. Its holding focuses heavier on corporate bonds than PDO, so it provides further diversification.

Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income (PTA) – Its currently trading at a -3.48% discount to NAV, making it appealing with an annual distribution yield of 7.84%. The combination of preferreds and fixed income make it ideal for income investors. Cohen & Steers tend to hold the monthly distribution constant for a long time. PTA has been paying $0.134 per share for the past three years.

Western Asset Diversified Income Fund (WDI) which is part of Franklin Templeton, is a closed end fund focused on income from fixed income securities including investment grade and high yield corporate securities, senior loans, residential and commercial mortgage-backed securities. Its current annual yield is 11.9%, paying distributions monthly.

Master Limited Partnerships - MLPs

Note, the first two MLPs use K-1s for tax reporting, so I’ve added a third option that invests in the space. The two MLPs chosen for the fund are the top two mentioned names on Seeking Alpha in this space. What I personally appreciate about them is their rising dividends. Going forward, the fund not only pays today but will increase payments in the future. Having a portion of the portfolio that is growing, not just distributing the same amount of income, is important to offset any negatives, and grow both the income potential and capital appreciation over time.

Enterprise Products Partners L.P. Common Units (EPD) – currently pays a 6.89% distribution yield, which historically grows over time. Seeking Alpha shows a Buy from Analysts and Wall Street. The chart below shows a gain of +84% over 5 years. Note, EPD’s distributions are classified as mostly return of capital vs. ordinary income. EPD uses a K-1 for tax reporting at year end.

MPLX LP Common Units (MPLX) – currently pays a 7.83% distribution and is rated Buy from SA Analysts, and Wall Street Analysts. Chart shows a price increase of +179% over 5 years. Note, distributions are classified as mostly return of capital vs. ordinary income. MPLX uses a K-1 for tax reporting at year end.

FT Energy Income Partners Enhanced Income ETF (EIPI) is an actively managed fund that invests at least 80% of its assets in Energy companies such as MLPs including EPD 8%, Energy Transfer LP (ET) 6.0%, MPLX 4.6%, Kinder Morgan Inc (KMI) 3.8%, Williams Companies Inc. (WMB) 3.9%, Shell (SHEL) 3.4%, and Exxon (XOM) 3.3%. In addition to distributing income from dividends, the fund earns premium income from writing covered calls on individual securities within the portfolio.

I recently covered EIPI in an article titled "EIPI: Monthly Income From MLPs Without The K-1,” where I rated the ETF a Buy. Click on the title to read more.

It currently pays a fixed dividend of $0.125 per share on a monthly basis, with a potential special dividend in December. On a trailing 12 month basis the yield works out to be 8.7%.

If you want to avoid K-1s, this is a good alternative to invest in vs. buying individual shares of EPD or MPLX, however distributions are taxed as ordinary income.

Dividend Income Funds

For these funds, distributions come from dividends, which are qualified in most cases (long term capital gains). These equity-based funds help diversify from fixed income, mortgage REITs and other interest rate sensitive products.

BlackRock Health Sciences Trust (BME) is invested in large health care stocks, many of which have been out of favor as the current administration puts pressure on drug prices. Top holdings include Eli Lilly (LLY), Boston Scientific (BSX), AbbVie (ABBV), UnitedHealth Group (UNH), Abbott Labs (ABT), Stryker (SYK), Danaher (DHR), and Thermo Fisher (TMO).

BME yields 8.28% with monthly distributions and trades at an -8.26% discount to NAV, which represents a Buy in my view.

Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (EVT) – Currently pays an 8.0% annual dividend yield and trades at a -7.63% discount to NAV. The stocks are primarily U.S. domestic equities like the S&P500 index.

The CEF pays a monthly distribution while still growing in price over time, making it an ideal investment for retirement, or investors that are taking the income vs. reinvesting it, in my opinion. In tracking the total return over a long period such as 10 years (+182%), the investment performs much better than a bond because its distributions are higher and the value of the principal continues to grow.

Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund (ETO) - currently yields a 7.42% forward yield, with an -8.69% discount to NAV. Although the yield appears low, the discount makes this closed end fund very attractive. Over time, ETO has been one of the top performing Eaton Vance CEFS with a 10 year total return of 196%.

I recently covered this CEF in an article titled "ETO: Monthly Income From Global Dividends, Trading At A Discount.” Click on the title to read more.

Cohen & Steers Closed-End Opportunity Fund (FOF)– this is a fund of funds which hold a highly diverse set of investments including bonds from PIMCO, commodities such as gold and silver with Sprott, and various BlackRock Capital allocation funds. What I like about this fund is that it pays a monthly distribution yielding 7.9% annually, and that it is invested in unusual funds that include commodities plus a set of PIMCO funds that I don’t already own. Another fund to consider is Saba Closed-End Funds ETF (CEFS), which has a similar fund of fund style; however it also has activist attention, which occasionally leads to outperformance.

Option (Derivative) Income

One of the fastest growing segments of income funds are ETFs or CEFs that use derivatives or options. After many years of trading options for income myself, I find investing in these funds a relief, as they do the work for you. The NEOS family of funds are especially interesting because they use tax advantaged index options classified as section 1256 contracts. Instead of being taxed at short term, ordinary income rates, these contracts automatically get taxed as 60% long term and 40% short term capital gains.

NEOS Bitcoin High Income ETF (BTCI) Full disclosure, this is the first fund I’ve ever invested in that holds Bitcoin or any type of cryptocurrency. The way I saw it, this fund generates income primarily from the volatility of Bitcoin, so I use it as an options trading vehicle. The actual Bitcoin holdings only account for 26% of the portfolio. This means that if Bitcoin fell in value, the fund could still make money on the volatility.

Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Global Buy-Write Opportunities Fund (ETW) – the fund has a call writing component over global equities, currently yielding 8.7%, paying monthly distributions. ETW trades at a -8.40% discount to NAV, which is a Buy in my view.

NEOS Russell 2000 High Income ETF (IWMI) is a diversifying fund from the two above using small cap stocks in the Russell 2000 Index (IWM) using an options overlay to generate income. It currently yields 14.27%, paying monthly distributions similar to the other two funds from NEOS.

JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) – similar to QQQI, the fund writes covered calls on the NASDAQ (QQQ), currently yielding 9.28%. It offers both high monthly income and the possibility of capital appreciation.

NEOS NASDAQ-100® High Income ETF (QQQI) – same as SPYI, but uses the Nasdaq (QQQ) with an options overlay to generate income. It will get close to the same total return but slightly underperform the index due to the exercise of call options. The fund currently pays a 13.99% annual yield.

NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI) – Full disclosure, this is one of my absolute favorite income funds because it’s monthly, pays a high dividend yield of 12%, is tax favorable, and can create capital appreciation through its holdings of the S&P 500 index.

Utility and Infrastructure Funds

Utility and infrastructure funds are ideal for generating income given their long term contracts and customers that are fairly locked into contracts. Recently the US has been focused on infrastructure initiatives which are giving companies an extra lift in earnings and valuations.

John Hancock Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (HTD) is a closed-end fund paying a monthly distribution yielding 7.6%, and trades at a 6.6% discount to NAV, which makes it a Buy in my view. HTD is composed of equities primarily utilities and financials paying qualified dividends, which provides a tax advantage. HTD achieves a strong 5 year (79%) and 10 year total return (165%), which preserves principal beyond the distributions. One of my favorite features of this fund is the growth of distributions over time.

abrdn Global Infrastructure Income Fund (ASGI) – currently yields 11.8%, paid out on a monthly basis. The holdings include NextEra, Norfolk Southern, American Tower Corp. and other recognizable names.

Real Estate Income Trusts - REITs

The model portfolio includes two funds involved in real estate.

Starwood Property Trust, Inc. (STWD) – is a hybrid REIT, paying a quarterly distribution of $0.48, which calculates to a 10.2% annual yield. The price recently dropped creating a buying opportunity with high yield. Normally it trades with a yield of approximately 9-10%. The REIT is diversifying beyond commercial mortgages to owning net lease properties, which reduces interest rate risk.

Cohen & Steers Real Estate Opportunities and Income Fund (RLTY) This closed end fund contains the names of blue-chip REITs such as Welltower, American Tower Corp, Digital Realty Trust, Simon Property Group, Prologis, and Iron Mountain. The annual distribution yield is currently 8.7% and trades at a -5.77% discount to NAV, which makes it a Buy in my view..

Performance of Each Source of Income

For BDCs, $10,000 invested over 10 years shows FDUS as the leader with $42,160 at the end. Over time, BDCs generate among the highest total returns over time in this portfolio. I did not include KBDC in this chart because it doesn’t have 10 years of history.

For the Bond funds, $10,000 invested since 2021, ARDC stands out by a wide margin, growing to $14,720 on a total return basis.

For the MLPs, $10,000 invested over 10 years shows the growth of both EPD and MPLX. EIPI only has a few years of history but since inception, it underperforms the other two funds as it is diversified with less high flyers.

For the Dividend Income ETFs, $10,000 invested since 2015, shows ETO outperforming peers growing to $29.7k. As an income investor, these charts are particularly appealing, in my view, because they show positive growth in overall return. In other words, the investment is generating capital appreciation with income.

For the Option Income, $10,000 invested over the past year shows the power of trading options on Bitcoin, as BTCI leads head and shoulders about the rest on total return.

For the Utilities, $10,000 invested over 5 years, shows ASGI having an edge in total return.

For the REITs, $10,000 invested in STWD and RLTY over the past 3 years shows Cohen & Steers RLTY well ahead of STWD.

Risks To Rating

In any portfolio structure, it’s important to consider the individual investor’s goals and risk tolerance. Many of the investments I’ve mentioned above are sensitive to interest rates and may be volatile at times. Doing this type of analysis makes it clearer on which type of investments are more attractive long term.

Diversification is the only free lunch. While it would be great to choose the best, highest performing securities on this list, they may not always act like that. In my experience, keeping every position to 5% or less reduces a stressful impact on the portfolio caused by a particular sector.

The stock market is currently trading at record level highs with many highly visible stocks trading at rich valuations. The potential for a correction is high, in my view. Although the market is shrugging off concerns about a government shutdown, it could prove to be a catalyst for a sell-off if not settled in a timely manner.

Bottom Line

I rate the following funds a Buy right now: BME, BTCI, EPD, ETO, ETW, FDUS, HTD, IWMI, MPLX, RLTY, and STWD.

The model portfolio I’ve outlined above can serve as a list of ideas to choose from, or a way to buy each one to build a portfolio of income producing securities. I identified this list from my own portfolio, dropping the losers and focusing on the high achievers. Ultimately, I recommend choosing a basket of 25 or more securities to diversify the portfolio. Having various sources of income from say business development companies, to dividend paying equities and bonds is another way to hedge what might happen in the macroenvironment.

I hope you found the article helpful. I really enjoy sharing ideas with fellow investors to make improvements and maximize performance. Please share your comments below. Thanks for reading.

data.title: Zillow: FTC Creates Overhang Over Critical Rentals Business (Downgrade)
data.description:
For the most part, large-cap tech stocks have enjoyed an aggressive late summer rally, buoyed by the prospect of rate cuts and continued crowding into the AI trade. One major holdout is Zillow (NASDAQ:Z), the self-described housing super-app.

Zillow stock has fallen ~20% from YTD highs near $90, technically putting the stock in bear market territory. The core driving reason behind the fallout is the FTC's lawsuit against Zillow, particularly in the high-growth rentals business that has carried Zillow's revenue amid a weaker for-sale housing market. The question for investors now is: Can Zillow rebound from here?

I last wrote a buy article on Zillow in August, when the stock was trading near $85 and before the lawsuit had been announced. While I typically think lawsuits are a mere distraction for most companies, I am particularly concerned about fallout impacts that the FTC suit could have on Zillow's Rentals business, which has become the lifeblood of the company's growth. Considering the substantial added uncertainty on this stock, I'm downgrading my position on Zillow to "Neutral."

The FTC suit and why it matters for Zillow's rentals segment

For investors who are unfamiliar, let's briefly round up the major arguments in the FTC's lawsuit against Zillow and potential fallout impacts for Zillow's financials.

In September, the FTC sued Zillow for allegedly paying $100 million to Redfin to exit the rentals business. As most investors are aware, Redfin is now a subsidiary of the Rocket Companies (RKT), which also acquired the Mr. Cooper Group to build a more holistic real estate company with a full brokerage, title, escrow, and mortgage business. The Zillow deal turned Redfin into an aggregator of Zillow's rental listings. Zillow framed the deal as a partnership, but the FTC is claiming it as anticompetitive behavior.

We'll avoid speculating on the case and potential outcomes, since it's a major unknown. The suit is on pause right now amid the ongoing government shutdown, though we note that this provides little respite for investors, as the suit is likely to get picked back up once the government resumes funding.

What we will focus on is the criticality of Zillow's rentals platform to its overall growth strategy. The chart below showcases Zillow's summary results for the first half of FY 2025:

Rentals revenue in the first half grew 35% y/y to $288 million in revenue, which is now 23% of Zillow's overall revenue. However, it represents half of the company's ~$150 million (14%) of y/y revenue growth and has been a larger contributor to growth than the company's core Premier Agent/Residential segment.

Rentals is also more than just a growing piece of Zillow's financials but a core portion of its product strategy. Many headlines this year have focused on the growing popularity of renting, particularly in age demographics that would in the past have entered first homebuying age. This is a function of not only housing unaffordability but also the desire for flexibility to move that has prevailed in our culture since COVID. In this environment, Zillow has sought not to lose mindshare as the first app that consumers go to for all things housing, so its pivot in Rentals has secured its spot as that one-stop shop for real estate.

The outcome of the FTC suit could lead to a sizable settlement, an injunction against anticompetitive behavior, or a rise in rentals competition. No matter what the outcome, it's an added distraction to the growth story for Zillow, especially when Zillow is already dealing with a lawsuit from Compass. As a reminder, the Compass lawsuit stands on similar anticompetitive grounds, accusing Zillow of retaliating against Compass agents on the Zillow platform for their use of private "pocket listings" (whereas Zillow wants every available house to be listed on the MLS and searchable on Zillow's site and app).

Valuation is on the expensive side, given uncertainty

Previously, I had argued that Zillow traded at a relatively rich but fair valuation given its growth profile. Yes, Zillow shares have dropped over the past month, but in my view, not enough to compensate for the added uncertainty for its most critical growth segment.

At current share prices near $71, Zillow trades at a market cap of $17.12 billion. Netting off the $1.16 billion of cash and $230 million of debt on Zillow's latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $16.19 billion.

For FY 2025, Zillow is guiding to mid-teens revenue growth. Wall Street consensus pegs this guidance at $2.57 billion in revenue, or 15% y/y growth.

The company is also guiding to "adjusted EBITDA margin expansion" for the full year. If we assume 1 point of y/y expansion (in line with margin expansion trends for the first half of the year) to a 23% full-year margin, adjusted EBITDA would be $591.1 million, up 19% y/y.

This positions Zillow's valuation at 27.4x EV/FY 2025 adjusted EBITDA. Now, for a company that is poised to hit double-digit revenue growth and has support from a potential recovery in the housing market, I'd consider that a fair multiple; and yet, when we stack on uncertainty from not one, but two pending lawsuits, I'm rendered skeptical about this premium.

Key takeaways

Of course, it's not all bad news for Zillow. The prospect of lower interest rates and a hopeful decline in mortgage rates may finally move many prospective homebuyers off the sidelines, putting the focus back on Zillow's core Residential segment. The potential for a rebound in brokerage activity is what keeps me from being overly bearish on this stock; hence, my "Neutral" rating.

At the same time, I'm hesitant to enter a fresh position in Zillow at its ~27x adjusted EBITDA multiple or to buy the dip in this stock when there's the possibility of a large fine or headwind to growth in its Rentals segment. I'm waiting on much more clarity in this name before diving back in; to me, it's best to be on the sidelines for now.
